# Who made this, and how carefully

<!-- rigor:summary -->
**The idea was mine and was worked in depth, over iterations. The plan was mine, developed with an AI and was worked in depth, over iterations. The implementation was generated by an AI. I have run and skimmed this code, but I have not read it properly. No human has understood it line by line. It was reviewed for quality (by an AI), reviewed for security (by an AI) and tested (by an AI). A human drives changes today. This assessment is as of 2026-07-07. I make no recommendation either way about depending on it.**
<!-- /rigor:summary -->

## Notes

The idea and intent behind this project is mine (human) and the plan and spec, human and AI collaboration.
I put real effort into the underlying design and the "why?". 

But the implementation, the CLI tool and the docs, I delegated in full to Claude Code (`stages.implementation.by: ai`), 
and I have only glanced at it superficially, hence `checks.comprehended: no`.

The implementation itself changed since the last assessment: the original Crystal CLI has been
retired and replaced, in full, by a fresh AI-written JavaScript port (same delegation, same
`implementation.by: ai` — this is a rewrite of AI-written code by AI, not a human taking over).
I did not write or hand-check the port line by line myself, so `checks.comprehended` stays `no`.

Also:

- **quality_reviewed / security_reviewed: ai** — the port was carried out and reviewed task by
  task, each task's diff put through a dedicated AI quality review and a dedicated AI security
  review before moving to the next. The security-sensitive corners of the original Crystal
  tool — YAML anchor/alias ("billion laughs") rejection, the stamp size cap, and the
  line-scanner that avoids pathological-input regex backtracking — were not assumed safe by
  inheritance; they were deliberately re-implemented in JavaScript and re-tested, since none of
  that hardening carries over for free across a language rewrite.
- **tested: ai** — the Crystal test suite was transcribed, task by task, into a Node.js
  (`node --test`) suite covering the same validator semantics, summary/README-line composition,
  and formatting — 144 tests in total at the point the byte-parity gate below last ran green.
  Behavior was then locked down further by a byte-parity
  gate: 39 representative stamps (fixtures plus a generated matrix across every rigor level,
  vouch form, and several deliberate violations) run through both the old Crystal binary and the
  new JS port and asserted identical — same exit codes, same `validate --json` output, same
  `embed`/`fmt` bytes — before the Crystal source was deleted. That gate passed with zero
  mismatches; its 39 tests now report as skipped rather than deleted, since the Crystal binary
  they compared against no longer exists to compare against.

I have not comprehended the code myself, and I am not claiming anyone else should be depending on
it, so `vouch` is **neutral**. 

No recommendation either way on its use by third parties.

## Stamp

```yaml
spec: "0.3"
rigor: skimmed
vouch: neutral
checks:
  comprehended: no
  quality_reviewed: ai
  security_reviewed: ai
  tested: ai
stages:
  idea: {by: human, depth: deep}
  plan: {by: human-with-ai, depth: deep}
  implementation: {by: ai}
  maintenance: {by: human}
assessed: 2026-07-07
```
